title: React & Next.js — The Complete Mastery Encyclopedia
body:
React & Next.js
Mastery Encyclopedia

Every concept, every hook, every pattern, every interview question — beginner to master level.

React 19.2 Next.js 16.2 100+ Interview Q&A

React is a JavaScript library for building UIs with components. React 19 is the current version. The mental model in 2026: server-first, compiler-optimized, less useEffect.

What React Actually Is

React is a declarative, component-based library for building user interfaces. You describe WHAT the UI should look like for given data, and React figures out HOW to update the screen efficiently. It uses a Virtual DOM — a lightweight JS representation of the real DOM — to batch and minimize actual DOM updates.

React is NOT a framework. It handles the view layer only. For routing, state management, SSR, and build tooling, you need additional libraries or a framework like Next.js.

React is like a recipe card system. Each card (component) describes what to display given certain ingredients (props/state). When ingredients change, React compares the new card with the old one and updates only what's different on the plate (DOM).

JSX — In Depth

JSX is syntactic sugar for React.createElement() calls. It looks like HTML but it's JavaScript.

// This JSX:
<h1 className="title">Hello, {name}!</h1>

// Compiles to:
React.createElement('h1', { className: 'title' }, 'Hello, ', name, '!');

// JSX Rules:
// 1. Single root element — use Fragment (<>...) to avoid extra divs
return (
  <>
    <h1>Title</h1>
    <p>Body</p>
  </>
);

// 2. All tags must close: , 
, 

// 3. camelCase for attributes:
//    class → className
//    for → htmlFor
//    onclick → onClick
//    tabindex → tabIndex
//    style takes an OBJECT, not a string:
<div style={{ color: 'red', fontSize: '16px' }}>

// 4. Expressions in { } — any valid JS expression
{isLoggedIn && <Welcome />}            // short-circuit
{role === 'admin' ? <Admin /> : <User />}  // ternary
{items.map(item => (                    // map (parentheses for implicit return!)
  <li key={item.id}>{item.name}</li>
))}

// 5. Spreading props
const props = { type: 'submit', disabled: true };
<button {...props}>Submit</button>

// 6. Rendering nothing: return null, undefined, or false
{showBanner && <Banner />} // renders nothing if showBanner is false
NEVER use index as key in lists: items.map((item, i) => <li key={i}>) causes bugs when items are reordered, filtered, or deleted. Always use a stable unique ID like item.id.

Components — The Building Blocks

Components are JavaScript functions that return JSX. They can receive props (inputs) and manage state (internal data).

// Basic component with props
function Greeting({ name, age = 25 }: { name: string; age?: number }) {
  return <p>Hello, {name}! Age: {age}</p>;
}

// With children prop
function Card({ title, children }: { title: string; children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <div className="card">
      <h2>{title}</h2>
      <div className="body">{children}</div>
    </div>
  );
}

// Usage: children is whatever you put between the tags
<Card title="Profile">
  <p>This is the card body!</p>
  <button>Edit</button>
</Card>

Props Rules

1. Props are read-only — never modify them. 2. Props flow one way (parent → child). 3. Default values: use destructuring defaults ({ count = 0 }). 4. TypeScript: always define prop types with interfaces or types.

Component Composition vs Inheritance

React strongly favours composition over inheritance. You never extend component classes. Instead, you compose components by nesting, passing children, or using render props.

Components are like LEGO blocks. Small blocks (Button, Input) snap together to form bigger blocks (LoginForm), which snap together to form even bigger structures (AuthPage). You never modify a LEGO piece — you combine them.

State — Making Components Interactive

import { useState } from 'react';

function Counter() {
  // useState returns [currentValue, setterFunction]
  const [count, setCount] = useState(0);

  return (
    <div>
      <p>Count: {count}</p>
      <button onClick={() => setCount(count + 1)}>+1</button>
      <button onClick={() => setCount(prev => prev + 1)}>+1 (safe)</button>
      <button onClick={() => setCount(0)}>Reset</button>
    </div>
  );
}

Critical State Rules

// 1. State updates are ASYNCHRONOUS and BATCHED
setCount(1);
setCount(2);
setCount(3);
// React batches these → only ONE re-render with count = 3

// 2. Use FUNCTIONAL UPDATES for state derived from previous state
// ❌ BUG: both read the same stale 'count'
setCount(count + 1);
setCount(count + 1); // still just +1 total

// ✅ CORRECT: each reads the latest pending value
setCount(prev => prev + 1);
setCount(prev => prev + 1); // +2 total

// 3. State is IMMUTABLE — never mutate directly
// ❌ WRONG: mutating state directly
user.name = 'Bob';
setUser(user); // React won't re-render! Same reference.

// ✅ CORRECT: create a new object
setUser({ ...user, name: 'Bob' });

// 4. Updating arrays immutably
setItems(prev => [...prev, newItem]);              // add
setItems(prev => prev.filter(i => i.id !== id));   // remove
setItems(prev => prev.map(i =>                     // update one
  i.id === id ? { ...i, done: true } : i
));
setItems(prev => prev.toSorted((a, b) => a.age - b.age)); // sort (new array)

// 5. Updating nested objects
setPerson(prev => ({
  ...prev,
  address: { ...prev.address, city: 'London' }
}));

// 6. Lazy initialization (expensive computation)
const [data, setData] = useState(() => {
  return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('data') || '[]');
}); // function only runs on FIRST render
When you call setState, React does NOT update the variable immediately. It schedules a re-render. On the next render, the component function runs again and useState returns the new value. This is why count appears "stale" immediately after setCount — you're still in the current render's closure.

Event Handling

// React events are camelCase and receive SyntheticEvent
<button onClick={handleClick}>Click</button>
<input onChange={handleChange} />
<form onSubmit={handleSubmit}>

// Pass event object
function handleClick(e: React.MouseEvent<HTMLButtonElement>) {
  e.preventDefault();  // prevent default behavior
  e.stopPropagation(); // stop event bubbling
  console.log(e.currentTarget); // the element the handler is on
  console.log(e.target);        // the actual element that was clicked
}

// Passing arguments to handlers
// ❌ BAD: creates new function every render
<button onClick={() => deleteItem(id)}>Delete</button>

// ✅ BETTER: use data attributes or currying
<button data-id={id} onClick={handleDelete}>Delete</button>
function handleDelete(e) {
  const id = e.currentTarget.dataset.id;
}

// Form handling
function handleSubmit(e: React.FormEvent<HTMLFormElement>) {
  e.preventDefault();
  const formData = new FormData(e.currentTarget);
  const name = formData.get('name');
}

Conditional Rendering — All Patterns

// 1. if/else (before return)
if (isLoading) return <Spinner />;
if (error) return <Error message={error} />;
return <Data />;

// 2. Ternary (inline)
{isLoggedIn ? <Dashboard /> : <Login />}

// 3. Short-circuit && (show or nothing)
{hasNotifications && <Badge count={count} />}

// ⚠️ GOTCHA: 0 &&  renders "0" not nothing!
{items.length && <List />} // renders "0" when empty!
{items.length > 0 && <List />} // ✅ correct

// 4. Null/undefined — renders nothing
return shouldShow ? <Widget /> : null;

// 5. Switch-like pattern with object mapping
const statusComponents = {
  loading: <Spinner />,
  error: <Error />,
  success: <Data />,
};
return statusComponents[status] ?? <Fallback />;

Rendering Lists — The Complete Guide

// Basic list
{users.map(user => (
  <UserCard key={user.id} user={user} />
))}

// With index (only when items are static + never reordered)
{staticLabels.map((label, index) => (
  <span key={index}>{label}</span>
))}

// Nested lists
{categories.map(cat => (
  <div key={cat.id}>
    <h2>{cat.name}</h2>
    {cat.items.map(item => (
      <Item key={item.id} data={item} />
    ))}
  </div>
))}

// Filtering + mapping (chain them!)
{todos
  .filter(t => !t.completed)
  .map(t => <TodoItem key={t.id} todo={t} />)
}
Why keys matter: When React re-renders a list, it uses keys to match old elements with new ones. Without keys (or with index keys), React can't tell if item #3 moved to position #5 — it destroys and recreates DOM nodes unnecessarily. With stable keys, React reuses and reorders existing nodes. This is critical for performance AND preserving component state (input values, scroll position).

Hooks are functions that let you "hook into" React features. Master every hook and you master React.

useState — Complete Reference

// Primitive state
const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
const [name, setName] = useState('');
const [isOpen, setIsOpen] = useState(false);

// Object state
const [user, setUser] = useState<User | null>(null);

// Lazy initial state (function runs only on first render)
const [todos, setTodos] = useState(() => {
  return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('todos') ?? '[]');
});

// Common patterns:
setIsOpen(prev => !prev);          // toggle
setItems(prev => [...prev, item]); // append
setUser(prev => prev ? { ...prev, name } : prev); // safe update

useEffect — The Most Misunderstood Hook

// useEffect synchronizes your component with external systems
// It is NOT "a lifecycle method" — it's a synchronization tool

// Pattern 1: Run on EVERY render (no dependency array)
useEffect(() => {
  console.log('Runs after every render');
});

// Pattern 2: Run ONCE on mount (empty array)
useEffect(() => {
  const ws = new WebSocket(url);
  ws.onmessage = handleMessage;
  return () => ws.close(); // cleanup on unmount
}, []);

// Pattern 3: Run when DEPENDENCIES change
useEffect(() => {
  const controller = new AbortController();

  fetch(`/api/users/${userId}`, { signal: controller.signal })
    .then(r => r.json())
    .then(setUser)
    .catch(err => {
      if (err.name !== 'AbortError') setError(err);
    });

  return () => controller.abort(); // cleanup: abort pending request
}, [userId]); // re-run when userId changes

// Pattern 4: Event listeners
useEffect(() => {
  const handler = (e: KeyboardEvent) => {
    if (e.key === 'Escape') onClose();
  };
  window.addEventListener('keydown', handler);
  return () => window.removeEventListener('keydown', handler);
}, [onClose]);

When NOT to use useEffect

// ❌ DON'T: Transform data for rendering (use useMemo or just compute)
useEffect(() => {
  setFilteredItems(items.filter(i => i.active));
}, [items]);
// ✅ INSTEAD: derive during render
const filteredItems = items.filter(i => i.active);
// or: const filteredItems = useMemo(() => items.filter(...), [items]);

// ❌ DON'T: Reset state when props change
useEffect(() => { setComment(''); }, [postId]);
// ✅ INSTEAD: use key to remount
<CommentForm key={postId} />

// ❌ DON'T: Handle user events
useEffect(() => {
  if (submitted) sendData();
}, [submitted]);
// ✅ INSTEAD: call function from event handler directly
const handleSubmit = () => sendData();
2026 Rule: "If you're reaching for useEffect, ask why first." The React team says useEffect should be a last resort, not the default for data fetching, derived state, or event responses. Use Server Components (fetch on server), event handlers (user interactions), or derived state (compute during render) instead.

useRef — Mutable Container

// 1. Access DOM elements
const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null);
// In template: 
// Later: inputRef.current?.focus();

// 2. Store mutable values that DON'T trigger re-renders
const intervalRef = useRef<NodeJS.Timeout>();
const prevValueRef = useRef(value);
const renderCountRef = useRef(0);
renderCountRef.current++; // no re-render!

// 3. Store latest callback without stale closure issues
const callbackRef = useRef(callback);
callbackRef.current = callback; // always up to date

// 4. Callback refs (when you need to know when a ref is set)
const measuredRef = useCallback((node: HTMLDivElement | null) => {
  if (node) {
    setHeight(node.getBoundingClientRect().height);
  }
}, []);
useRef is like a sticky note on your desk. You can write on it, erase it, change it — and nobody else notices. It's your private scratchpad that persists between renders but never causes a re-render when changed.

useContext — Global State Without Prop Drilling

// 1. Create context
interface ThemeCtx { theme: 'light' | 'dark'; toggle: () => void; }
const ThemeContext = createContext<ThemeCtx | null>(null);

// 2. Create provider component
function ThemeProvider({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  const [theme, setTheme] = useState<'light' | 'dark'>('dark');
  const toggle = () => setTheme(t => t === 'dark' ? 'light' : 'dark');
  return (
    <ThemeContext.Provider value={{ theme, toggle }}>
      {children}
    </ThemeContext.Provider>
  );
}

// 3. Custom hook (type-safe, fails fast)
function useTheme() {
  const ctx = useContext(ThemeContext);
  if (!ctx) throw new Error('useTheme must be used within ThemeProvider');
  return ctx;
}

// 4. Consume anywhere
function Header() {
  const { theme, toggle } = useTheme();
  return <button onClick={toggle}>Current: {theme}</button>;
}
Context limitation: When the context value changes, ALL consuming components re-render, even if they only use a small part of the value. For frequently changing values (mouse position, real-time data), use external state management (Zustand, Jotai) instead.

useReducer — Complex State Logic

interface State { items: Item[]; loading: boolean; error: string | null; }
type Action =
  | { type: 'FETCH_START' }
  | { type: 'FETCH_SUCCESS'; payload: Item[] }
  | { type: 'FETCH_ERROR'; payload: string }
  | { type: 'ADD_ITEM'; payload: Item }
  | { type: 'DELETE_ITEM'; payload: string };

function reducer(state: State, action: Action): State {
  switch (action.type) {
    case 'FETCH_START':
      return { ...state, loading: true, error: null };
    case 'FETCH_SUCCESS':
      return { ...state, loading: false, items: action.payload };
    case 'FETCH_ERROR':
      return { ...state, loading: false, error: action.payload };
    case 'ADD_ITEM':
      return { ...state, items: [...state.items, action.payload] };
    case 'DELETE_ITEM':
      return { ...state, items: state.items.filter(i => i.id !== action.payload) };
  }
}

const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, {
  items: [], loading: false, error: null
});

dispatch({ type: 'ADD_ITEM', payload: newItem });
Use useReducer over useState when: state has multiple sub-values, next state depends on previous state, state transitions are complex, or you want to pass dispatch down instead of multiple setter functions.

useMemo & useCallback

// useMemo: memoize a COMPUTED VALUE
const sortedItems = useMemo(() => {
  return [...items].sort((a, b) => a.name.localeCompare(b.name));
}, [items]); // only recomputes when items change

// useCallback: memoize a FUNCTION REFERENCE
const handleDelete = useCallback((id: string) => {
  setItems(prev => prev.filter(i => i.id !== id));
}, []); // same function reference across renders

// When to use useCallback:
// 1. Passing callbacks to React.memo'd children
// 2. As useEffect dependencies
// 3. In context values

// When NOT to use useMemo/useCallback:
// 1. Simple computations (overhead > benefit)
// 2. With React 19 Compiler (it auto-memoizes!)
// 3. Values that always change anyway
With the React Compiler (React 19), useMemo and useCallback are automatically inserted by the compiler where needed. You can write plain code and the compiler optimizes it. These hooks become "escape hatches" for rare edge cases, not everyday tools.

React 19 New Hooks

// useActionState — form action state management
const [state, formAction, isPending] = useActionState(
  async (prevState, formData) => {
    const error = await createUser(formData);
    if (error) return { error };
    return { error: null };
  },
  { error: null }
);
<form action={formAction}>
  <input name="email" />
  {state.error && <p className="error">{state.error}</p>}
  <button disabled={isPending}>{isPending ? '...' : 'Submit'}</button>
</form>

// useOptimistic — instant UI updates before server confirms
const [optimisticTodos, addOptimistic] = useOptimistic(
  todos,
  (current, newTodo) => [...current, { ...newTodo, pending: true }]
);

async function handleAdd(formData) {
  addOptimistic({ text: formData.get('text'), pending: true });
  await addTodoToServer(formData); // if fails, optimistic update reverts
}

// use() — read Promises and Context in render
function UserProfile({ userPromise }) {
  const user = use(userPromise); // suspends component until resolved
  return <h1>{user.name}</h1>;
}
// Unlike useContext, use() can be called conditionally!
if (showTheme) {
  const theme = use(ThemeContext); // ✅ valid with use()
}

// useTransition — mark updates as non-urgent
const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition();
startTransition(async () => {
  await saveData(); // React 19: async transitions!
  navigate('/success');
});

Custom Hooks — Real-World Examples

// useDebounce — delay value changes
function useDebounce<T>(value: T, delay: number): T {
  const [debounced, setDebounced] = useState(value);
  useEffect(() => {
    const t = setTimeout(() => setDebounced(value), delay);
    return () => clearTimeout(t);
  }, [value, delay]);
  return debounced;
}

// useMediaQuery — responsive hooks
function useMediaQuery(query: string): boolean {
  const [matches, setMatches] = useState(
    () => window.matchMedia(query).matches
  );
  useEffect(() => {
    const mql = window.matchMedia(query);
    const handler = (e: MediaQueryListEvent) => setMatches(e.matches);
    mql.addEventListener('change', handler);
    return () => mql.removeEventListener('change', handler);
  }, [query]);
  return matches;
}

// useFetch — data fetching with loading/error
function useFetch<T>(url: string) {
  const [data, setData] = useState<T | null>(null);
  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);
  const [error, setError] = useState<string | null>(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    const ctrl = new AbortController();
    setLoading(true);
    fetch(url, { signal: ctrl.signal })
      .then(r => { if (!r.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${r.status}`); return r.json(); })
      .then(d => { setData(d); setError(null); })
      .catch(e => { if (e.name !== 'AbortError') setError(e.message); })
      .finally(() => setLoading(false));
    return () => ctrl.abort();
  }, [url]);

  return { data, loading, error };
}

// useOnClickOutside — detect clicks outside an element
function useOnClickOutside(ref: RefObject<HTMLElement>, handler: () => void) {
  useEffect(() => {
    const listener = (e: MouseEvent) => {
      if (!ref.current || ref.current.contains(e.target as Node)) return;
      handler();
    };
    document.addEventListener('mousedown', listener);
    return () => document.removeEventListener('mousedown', listener);
  }, [ref, handler]);
}

// usePrevious — get previous value of a prop/state
function usePrevious<T>(value: T): T | undefined {
  const ref = useRef<T>();
  useEffect(() => { ref.current = value; });
  return ref.current;
}

How data flows through your app determines its maintainability. Master these patterns.

State Management — The Full Spectrum

Level 1: Local State (useState)

For state that belongs to a single component. Form inputs, toggles, UI state.

Level 2: Lifted State

Move state UP to the nearest common parent when siblings need to share it. Pass down via props.

Level 3: Context + useReducer

For state needed by many components at different depths. Auth state, theme, locale. Combine with useReducer for complex transitions.

Level 4: Zustand (Lightweight Store)

import { create } from 'zustand';

const useStore = create((set) => ({
  count: 0,
  increment: () => set(s => ({ count: s.count + 1 })),
  reset: () => set({ count: 0 }),
}));

// Usage — no Provider wrapper needed!
function Counter() {
  const { count, increment } = useStore();
  return <button onClick={increment}>{count}</button>;
}

Level 5: TanStack Query (Server State)

import { useQuery, useMutation, useQueryClient } from '@tanstack/react-query';

// Fetch data with caching, refetching, stale-while-revalidate
function UserList() {
  const { data, isLoading, error } = useQuery({
    queryKey: ['users'],
    queryFn: () => fetch('/api/users').then(r => r.json()),
    staleTime: 60_000, // consider fresh for 1 min
  });

  if (isLoading) return <Spinner />;
  if (error) return <Error />;
  return data.map(u => <UserCard key={u.id} user={u} />);
}

// Mutate data with optimistic updates
const queryClient = useQueryClient();
const mutation = useMutation({
  mutationFn: (newUser) => fetch('/api/users', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify(newUser) }),
  onSuccess: () => queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['users'] }),
});
2026 best practice: Use TanStack Query for all server state (API data). Use Zustand for client state (UI state). This covers 95% of apps without Redux.

Lifting State Up vs Composition

// Problem: Two siblings need the same state
// Solution: Lift state to their parent

function Parent() {
  const [selected, setSelected] = useState<string | null>(null);

  return (
    <>
      <Sidebar items={items} selected={selected} onSelect={setSelected} />
      <Detail itemId={selected} />
    </>
  );
}

// Alternative: Composition (children pattern) to avoid prop drilling
function Layout({ sidebar, detail }: { sidebar: ReactNode; detail: ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <div className="flex">
      <aside>{sidebar}</aside>
      <main>{detail}</main>
    </div>
  );
}

// Parent controls what goes in each slot
<Layout
  sidebar={<Sidebar selected={selected} onSelect={setSelected} />}
  detail={<Detail itemId={selected} />}
/>

Architecture patterns that make React apps scalable and maintainable.

Component Patterns

1. Presentational vs Container

Presentational: Only renders UI. Receives data via props. No side effects. Container: Fetches data, manages state, passes to presentational components.

2. Compound Components

// Components that work together, sharing implicit state
const TabsContext = createContext();

function Tabs({ children, defaultTab }) {
  const [active, setActive] = useState(defaultTab);
  return (
    <TabsContext.Provider value={{ active, setActive }}>
      <div className="tabs">{children}</div>
    </TabsContext.Provider>
  );
}

Tabs.Tab = function Tab({ id, children }) {
  const { active, setActive } = useContext(TabsContext);
  return (
    <button className={active === id ? 'active' : ''}
            onClick={() => setActive(id)}>{children}</button>
  );
};

Tabs.Panel = function Panel({ id, children }) {
  const { active } = useContext(TabsContext);
  return active === id ? <div>{children}</div> : null;
};

// Clean, intuitive API:
<Tabs defaultTab="profile">
  <Tabs.Tab id="profile">Profile</Tabs.Tab>
  <Tabs.Tab id="settings">Settings</Tabs.Tab>
  <Tabs.Panel id="profile"><ProfileForm /></Tabs.Panel>
  <Tabs.Panel id="settings"><SettingsForm /></Tabs.Panel>
</Tabs>

3. Render Props (Legacy but still asked in interviews)

function MouseTracker({ render }) {
  const [pos, setPos] = useState({ x: 0, y: 0 });
  return (
    <div onMouseMove={e => setPos({ x: e.clientX, y: e.clientY })}>
      {render(pos)}
    </div>
  );
}
// Usage:
<MouseTracker render={({ x, y }) => <p>{x}, {y}</p>} />

// Modern alternative: custom hook
const pos = useMousePosition(); // much cleaner

4. Error Boundaries

// Still class-based (no hook equivalent yet)
class ErrorBoundary extends React.Component {
  state = { hasError: false, error: null };

  static getDerivedStateFromError(error) {
    return { hasError: true, error };
  }

  componentDidCatch(error, info) {
    logToService(error, info.componentStack);
  }

  render() {
    if (this.state.hasError) {
      return <Fallback error={this.state.error} />;
    }
    return this.props.children;
  }
}

// Usage: wrap around sections that might crash
<ErrorBoundary>
  <Dashboard />
</ErrorBoundary>

Project Structure (Feature-Based)

src/
├── app/                    # Next.js App Router pages
├── components/
│   ├── ui/                 # Reusable primitives (Button, Modal, Input)
│   └── features/           # Feature-specific components
│       ├── auth/
│       │   ├── LoginForm.tsx
│       │   ├── useAuth.ts  # custom hook
│       │   └── auth.api.ts # API functions
│       └── dashboard/
├── hooks/                  # Shared custom hooks
├── lib/                    # Utilities, helpers, constants
├── stores/                 # Zustand stores
├── types/                  # TypeScript types/interfaces
└── styles/                 # Global styles

React 19 is a paradigm shift. Server Components, the Compiler, Actions, and new hooks change how you build apps.

React Compiler ("React Forget")

A build-time tool that automatically memoizes components and values. It analyses your code and inserts the equivalent of useMemo/useCallback/React.memo where needed.

// BEFORE React Compiler (React 18 way)
const sorted = useMemo(() => [...items].sort(compareFn), [items]);
const handleClick = useCallback(() => onClick(id), [id, onClick]);
const MemoChild = React.memo(ExpensiveChild);

// AFTER React Compiler (React 19 way)
const sorted = [...items].sort(compareFn);   // compiler handles it
const handleClick = () => onClick(id);        // compiler handles it
// No React.memo needed — compiler decides
The compiler works by statically analysing your code at build time, identifying which values depend on which inputs, and inserting cache checks. It follows the Rules of React (pure render functions, no side effects during render) to determine what's safe to memoize. If your code violates these rules, the compiler emits warnings.

Server Components (RSC) — Deep Dive

// SERVER Component (default in Next.js App Router)
// ✅ Can: access DB, read files, use secrets, import large libs
// ❌ Cannot: useState, useEffect, onClick, browser APIs
async function ProductList() {
  const products = await db.query('SELECT * FROM products WHERE active = true');
  const formatted = formatPrices(products); // heavy lib stays on server

  return (
    <div>
      {formatted.map(p => (
        <ProductCard key={p.id} product={p} /> {/* can be server component */}
      ))}
      <AddToCartButton /> {/* client component for interactivity */}
    </div>
  );
}

// CLIENT Component (opt-in with 'use client')
'use client';
function AddToCartButton({ productId }) {
  const [added, setAdded] = useState(false);
  return (
    <button onClick={() => { addToCart(productId); setAdded(true); }}>
      {added ? '✓ Added' : 'Add to Cart'}
    </button>
  );
}
Server Components are the kitchen — heavy preparation (data fetching, formatting, large libraries) happens there and never reaches the customer (browser). Client Components are the dining room experience — interactive things the customer does (clicking buttons, filling forms, toggling menus).

Server vs Client — Decision Tree

Use Server Component when: fetching data, accessing backend resources, keeping sensitive info (API keys) on server, reducing client JS bundle, no interactivity needed.

Use Client Component when: you need useState, useEffect, event handlers (onClick, onChange), browser APIs (localStorage, geolocation), or third-party libraries that use React state.

Server Actions — Mutations Without API Routes

// actions.ts
'use server';

import { revalidatePath } from 'next/cache';
import { redirect } from 'next/navigation';
import { z } from 'zod';

const schema = z.object({
  title: z.string().min(1).max(100),
  content: z.string().min(10),
});

export async function createPost(prevState: any, formData: FormData) {
  const raw = Object.fromEntries(formData);
  const parsed = schema.safeParse(raw);

  if (!parsed.success) {
    return { errors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors };
  }

  await db.insert('posts', parsed.data);
  revalidatePath('/posts'); // bust cache
  redirect('/posts');        // navigate
}

// Component using the action
'use client';
import { useActionState } from 'react';
import { createPost } from './actions';

function CreatePostForm() {
  const [state, action, pending] = useActionState(createPost, { errors: {} });

  return (
    <form action={action}>
      <input name="title" />
      {state.errors?.title && <p className="error">{state.errors.title}</p>}
      <textarea name="content" />
      {state.errors?.content && <p className="error">{state.errors.content}</p>}
      <button disabled={pending}>{pending ? 'Publishing...' : 'Publish'}</button>
    </form>
  );
}
Server Actions work with progressive enhancement — forms still work even if JavaScript hasn't loaded yet. The form submits as a standard HTML form, and the server processes it. This is a massive accessibility and performance win.

React 19 Complete Feature List

  • React Compiler — Auto-memoization at build time
  • Server Components — Components that run only on the server
  • Server Actions — 'use server' for data mutations
  • Actions (async transitions) — startTransition can now accept async functions
  • useActionState — Manage form action state (pending, errors)
  • useOptimistic — Optimistic UI updates
  • use() — Read Promises and Context anywhere (conditional calls OK)
  • Document Metadata — <title>, <meta>, <link> directly in components
  • Stylesheet support — Manage CSS precedence in components
  • ref as prop — No more forwardRef needed
  • Improved hydration error messages — Shows exact diff of mismatches
  • Owner Stacks — Better debugging with component ownership in errors

Performance optimization is about understanding WHEN and WHY React re-renders.

Why Components Re-Render

A component re-renders when:

  • Its state changes (setState called)
  • Its parent re-renders (ALL children re-render by default)
  • A context value it consumes changes

A component does NOT re-render when:

  • Its props haven't changed AND it's wrapped in React.memo
  • A sibling re-renders (siblings are independent)
  • A ref changes

Optimization Strategies (Ranked by Impact)

// 1. MOVE STATE DOWN — keep state close to where it's used
// Bad: state in App causes EVERYTHING to re-render
// Good: state in SearchBar only re-renders SearchBar

// 2. LIFT CONTENT UP — children as props avoid re-renders
function ExpensiveLayout({ children }) {
  const [color, setColor] = useState('red');
  return (
    <div style={{ color }}>
      <button onClick={() => setColor('blue')}>Change</button>
      {children} {/* children DON'T re-render when color changes! */}
    </div>
  );
}

// 3. React.memo — skip re-render if props unchanged
const MemoizedList = React.memo(function UserList({ users }) {
  return users.map(u => <p key={u.id}>{u.name}</p>);
});

// 4. React.lazy + Suspense — code-split heavy components
const Chart = lazy(() => import('./Chart'));
<Suspense fallback={<Skeleton />}>
  <Chart data={data} />
</Suspense>

// 5. Virtualization — only render visible items
// Use @tanstack/react-virtual for 1000+ item lists

// 6. useTransition — defer expensive re-renders
const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition();
const handleSearch = (term) => {
  setInput(term);          // urgent: update input immediately
  startTransition(() => {
    setResults(filter(term)); // non-urgent: can be interrupted
  });
};

Next.js 16.2 is the latest (March 2026). It's the recommended way to build production React apps with Server Components, SSR, and full-stack features.

App Router — Complete File Conventions

app/
├── layout.tsx              # Root layout (required). Wraps ALL pages.
├── page.tsx                # Home page → /
├── loading.tsx             # Auto Suspense boundary for this route
├── error.tsx               # Error boundary for this route
├── not-found.tsx           # 404 page for notFound() calls
├── global-error.tsx        # Root-level error boundary
├── template.tsx            # Like layout but re-mounts on navigation
│
├── about/page.tsx          # → /about
├── blog/
│   ├── page.tsx            # → /blog
│   └── [slug]/page.tsx     # → /blog/:slug (dynamic)
├── shop/[...slug]/page.tsx # → /shop/a/b/c (catch-all)
├── (marketing)/            # Route group — no URL impact, shares layout
│   ├── layout.tsx
│   ├── about/page.tsx
│   └── contact/page.tsx
├── @modal/                 # Parallel route (named slot)
│   └── login/page.tsx
│
└── api/
    └── users/
        └── route.ts        # API route: GET/POST/PUT/DELETE /api/users

Special Files — Rendering Order

Next.js wraps your page in these layers (outermost to innermost):

<Layout>           # layout.tsx — persists across navigation
  <Template>       # template.tsx — re-mounts on navigation
    <ErrorBoundary> # error.tsx — catches errors
      <Suspense>    # loading.tsx — shows while loading
        <Page />    # page.tsx — your content
      </Suspense>
    </ErrorBoundary>
  </Template>
</Layout>

Layouts — Persistent, Nested UI

// app/layout.tsx — Root layout (REQUIRED)
import { Metadata } from 'next';
export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: { default: 'My App', template: '%s | My App' },
  description: 'The best app',
};

export default function RootLayout({ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <html lang="en">
      <body>
        <Providers>
          <Navbar />
          <main>{children}</main>
          <Footer />
        </Providers>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}

// app/dashboard/layout.tsx — Nested layout
export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) {
  return (
    <div className="flex">
      <Sidebar />          {/* persists as user navigates dashboard pages */}
      <section>{children}</section>
    </div>
  );
}
Layouts don't re-render when navigating between pages that share the same layout. State inside layouts (like sidebar open/close) persists. This is fundamentally different from React Router, where everything re-renders on navigation.

Dynamic Routes, Params & Search Params

// app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx — Dynamic segment
export default async function BlogPost({
  params,
}: {
  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>;
}) {
  const { slug } = await params; // params is async in Next.js 15+!
  const post = await getPost(slug);
  if (!post) notFound(); // renders not-found.tsx
  return <article><h1>{post.title}</h1></article>;
}

// Static generation for dynamic routes
export async function generateStaticParams() {
  const posts = await getAllPosts();
  return posts.map(p => ({ slug: p.slug }));
}

// Search params (query string)
export default async function SearchPage({
  searchParams,
}: {
  searchParams: Promise<{ q?: string; page?: string }>;
}) {
  const { q, page } = await searchParams;
  const results = await search(q, Number(page) || 1);
  return <ResultsList results={results} />;
}

// API Route Handlers
// app/api/users/[id]/route.ts
export async function GET(request: Request, { params }: { params: Promise<{ id: string }> }) {
  const { id } = await params;
  const user = await db.findUser(id);
  if (!user) return Response.json({ error: 'Not found' }, { status: 404 });
  return Response.json(user);
}

export async function DELETE(request: Request, { params }) {
  const { id } = await params;
  await db.deleteUser(id);
  return new Response(null, { status: 204 });
}

Next.js has the most flexible rendering model of any framework. Understanding when pages are static vs dynamic is critical.

Rendering Strategies — Complete Guide

1. Static Rendering (SSG) — Build Time

Pages are rendered at build time and cached. Served directly from CDN. Fastest possible delivery. Default for pages without dynamic data.

2. Dynamic Rendering (SSR) — Request Time

Pages are rendered on the server for each request. Used automatically when you access: cookies(), headers(), searchParams, or uncached fetch calls.

3. Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)

Static pages that revalidate in the background after a time interval. Best for content that changes periodically.

// Time-based revalidation
const data = await fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: 3600 } }); // every hour

// On-demand revalidation (in a Server Action)
revalidatePath('/blog');          // revalidate all posts
revalidateTag('blog-posts');      // revalidate by cache tag

4. Streaming SSR

Send HTML in chunks as it becomes ready. The user sees content progressively instead of waiting for everything. Enabled automatically with loading.tsx and Suspense boundaries.

5. Partial Prerendering (PPR) — Next.js 16

Combines static and dynamic in the same page. Static shell is prerendered and served from CDN. Dynamic parts are wrapped in Suspense and streamed when ready.

// The layout, header, and product grid are STATIC (instant)
// The user's cart and recommendations are DYNAMIC (streamed)
export default async function ProductPage() {
  return (
    <div>
      <Header />                         {/* static */}
      <ProductGrid products={products} /> {/* static */}
      <Suspense fallback={<CartSkeleton />}>
        <UserCart />                      {/* dynamic, streamed */}
      </Suspense>
      <Suspense fallback={<RecSkeleton />}>
        <Recommendations />               {/* dynamic, streamed */}
      </Suspense>
    </div>
  );
}

Caching in Next.js 16 — 'use cache'

// Next.js 16: ALL code is DYNAMIC by default
// Caching is explicitly OPT-IN with 'use cache'

// Cache an entire page
'use cache';
export default async function BlogPage() {
  const posts = await getPosts(); // cached!
  return <PostList posts={posts} />;
}

// Cache a component
async function PopularPosts() {
  'use cache';
  const posts = await getPopularPosts();
  return <Sidebar posts={posts} />;
}

// Cache a function
async function getSettings() {
  'use cache';
  return await db.query('SELECT * FROM settings');
}

// Control cache lifetime
import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache';
async function Dashboard() {
  'use cache';
  cacheLife('minutes'); // or 'hours', 'days', 'weeks', 'max'
  // ...
}
Old caching (Next.js 15) was like a fridge that automatically stores everything — confusing when you want fresh food. New caching (Next.js 16) is like you choose what goes in the fridge and for how long. Everything else stays fresh on the counter.

Data fetching and mutations in the App Router.

Data Fetching — Server Components

// Direct database access (NO API route needed)
export default async function UsersPage() {
  const users = await prisma.user.findMany({
    where: { active: true },
    orderBy: { name: 'asc' },
  });

  return (
    <ul>
      {users.map(u => <li key={u.id}>{u.name}</li>)}
    </ul>
  );
}

// Parallel data fetching
export default async function Dashboard() {
  // Start both fetches simultaneously
  const [users, metrics, notifications] = await Promise.all([
    getUsers(),
    getMetrics(),
    getNotifications(),
  ]);

  return (
    <>
      <UserList users={users} />
      <MetricsPanel data={metrics} />
      <NotificationBell items={notifications} />
    </>
  );
}

// Sequential data fetching (when one depends on another)
export default async function UserProfile({ params }) {
  const { id } = await params;
  const user = await getUser(id);
  const posts = await getPostsByUser(user.id); // depends on user
  return <Profile user={user} posts={posts} />;
}

Middleware — Complete Guide

// middleware.ts (project root, runs on EVERY matched request)
import { NextResponse } from 'next/server';
import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server';

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  // 1. Auth check
  const token = request.cookies.get('session')?.value;
  if (!token && request.nextUrl.pathname.startsWith('/dashboard')) {
    return NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/login', request.url));
  }

  // 2. Geolocation-based redirect
  const country = request.geo?.country || 'US';
  if (country === 'GB' && request.nextUrl.pathname === '/') {
    return NextResponse.redirect(new URL('/uk', request.url));
  }

  // 3. Add custom headers
  const response = NextResponse.next();
  response.headers.set('x-user-country', country);

  // 4. Rewrite URL (proxy to different path)
  // return NextResponse.rewrite(new URL('/api/proxy', request.url));

  return response;
}

export const config = {
  matcher: [
    '/((?!_next/static|_next/image|favicon.ico).*)', // all except static
  ],
};

Image & Font Optimization

// Image optimization — automatic WebP/AVIF, lazy loading, sizing
import Image from 'next/image';

<Image
  src="/hero.jpg"

  blurDataURL="data:..."   {/* base64 blur image */}

const outfit = Outfit({
  subsets: ['latin'],

  display: 'swap',
  variable: '--font-outfit',

// In layout:

Advanced patterns that separate junior from senior Next.js developers.

Parallel Routes & Intercepting Routes

// PARALLEL ROUTES — render multiple pages simultaneously
// Named slots with @folder convention
app/
├── layout.tsx          # receives { children, modal, sidebar } props
├── @modal/
│   └── login/page.tsx  # /login renders as a modal overlay
├── @sidebar/
│   └── default.tsx     # fallback when no match
└── page.tsx

// layout.tsx uses both slots
export default function Layout({ children, modal, sidebar }) {
  return (
    <>
      {sidebar}
      {children}
      {modal}
    </>
  );
}

// INTERCEPTING ROUTES — show a route as a modal
// (..) means intercept one level up
app/
├── feed/
│   ├── page.tsx
│   └── (..)photo/[id]/page.tsx  # intercepts /photo/:id as modal
└── photo/[id]/page.tsx          # full page version

Metadata & SEO

// Static metadata
export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: 'Blog — My App',
  description: 'Read our latest articles',
  keywords: ['react', 'nextjs'],
  openGraph: {
    title: 'Blog',
    description: 'Latest articles',
    images: [{ url: '/og-blog.png', width: 1200, height: 630 }],
  },
  twitter: { card: 'summary_large_image' },
  robots: { index: true, follow: true },
};

// Dynamic metadata (per-page, based on data)
export async function generateMetadata({ params }): Promise<Metadata> {
  const { slug } = await params;
  const post = await getPost(slug);
  return {
    title: post.title,
    description: post.excerpt,
    openGraph: { images: [post.coverImage] },
  };
}

// Sitemap generation
// app/sitemap.ts
export default async function sitemap(): Promise<MetadataRoute.Sitemap> {
  const posts = await getAllPosts();
  return [
    { url: 'https://myapp.com', lastModified: new Date(), priority: 1 },
    ...posts.map(p => ({
      url: `https://myapp.com/blog/${p.slug}`,
      lastModified: p.updatedAt,
    })),
  ];
}

Authentication Pattern

// lib/auth.ts — check auth in server components
import { cookies } from 'next/headers';
import { redirect } from 'next/navigation';

export async function getUser() {
  const cookieStore = await cookies();
  const session = cookieStore.get('session')?.value;
  if (!session) return null;
  return verifySession(session);
}

export async function requireAuth() {
  const user = await getUser();
  if (!user) redirect('/login');
  return user;
}

// Usage in any server component
export default async function DashboardPage() {
  const user = await requireAuth(); // redirects if not logged in
  return <h1>Welcome, {user.name}</h1>;
}

Next.js 16 and 16.2 (March 2026) — the latest features you must know.

Next.js 16 — Major Features

  • Turbopack stable & default — Rust-based bundler. 5-10x faster Fast Refresh, 2-5x faster builds
  • Cache Components + 'use cache' — Explicit opt-in caching. All code dynamic by default
  • Partial Prerendering (PPR) — Static shell + dynamic streaming in same page
  • React Compiler support — Built-in automatic memoization
  • proxy.ts — Network proxy replacing some middleware patterns
  • Adapter API (stable) — Deploy to any hosting provider, not just Vercel
  • DevTools MCP — AI agents can interact with Next.js diagnostics
  • Incremental prefetching — Only prefetch parts not already in cache

Next.js 16.2 — Latest (March 2026)

  • ~400% faster dev startup — localhost:3000 ready in milliseconds
  • ~50% faster rendering — RSC payload deserialization 350% faster
  • Server Fast Refresh — Fine-grained server-side hot reloading
  • AI Improvements — AGENTS.md scaffolding, browser log forwarding for AI agents, experimental Agent DevTools
  • SRI support — Subresource Integrity for JS files
  • 200+ Turbopack bug fixes
  • Turbopack file system caching (stable) — Compile artifacts stored on disk for faster restarts
The RSC performance gain comes from replacing JSON.parse with a reviver callback (which crosses V8's C++/JS boundary per key) with a two-step approach: plain JSON.parse() followed by a pure-JS recursive walk. This eliminates the boundary-crossing overhead entirely. Real-world impact: 25-60% faster HTML rendering.

CVE-2025-55182 — React2Shell (CRITICAL)

In November 2025, a vulnerability was reported allowing unauthenticated remote code execution through a flaw in React Server Component deserialization. CVSS score: 10.0 (maximum). Next.js 15.x and 16.x were affected. A fix was published December 3rd. Exploitation attempts were observed within hours of disclosure.

Always keep React and Next.js updated. Pin exact versions in production. Monitor security advisories. This CVE demonstrates that server-side rendering introduces server-side attack surfaces.
BASIC What is React? +

A declarative, efficient, and flexible JavaScript library for building user interfaces, primarily maintained by Meta (Facebook).

BASIC What is a component? +

Independent and reusable bits of code. They serve the same purpose as JavaScript functions, but work in isolation and return HTML (JSX) via a render function.

BASIC What are props? +

Short for properties. They are read-only components that must be kept pure. They are used to pass data from one component to another.

BASIC What is state in React? +

An object that holds some information that may change over the lifetime of the component. When state changes, the component responds by re-rendering.

BASIC What is the Virtual DOM? +

A lightweight JavaScript representation of the DOM. React updates the Virtual DOM first, compares it to the previous version (diffing), and then applies the minimal necessary changes to the real DOM (reconciliation).

BASIC What is reconciliation? +

The process through which React updates the Browser DOM. When a component’s state changes, React calculates the difference between the new and old Virtual DOMs and only updates the changed nodes.

BASIC What is the "key" prop? +

A special string attribute you need to include when creating lists of elements. Keys help React identify which items have changed, been added, or been removed, optimizing the reconciliation process.

BASIC What are React Hooks? +

Functions that let you "hook into" React state and lifecycle features from function components. They do not work inside classes.

BASIC What does useState do? +

It declares a state variable that you can update directly. It returns a pair: the current state value and a function that lets you update it.

BASIC What does useEffect do? +

It lets you perform side effects in function components, such as data fetching, subscriptions, or manually changing the DOM.

BASIC What is a functional component vs a class component? +

Function components are simpler, writing UI as pure functions. Class components require extending `React.Component` and using `this`. Hooks made function components the modern standard.

BASIC What is an event in React? +

React events are named using camelCase, rather than lowercase. With JSX you pass a function as the event handler, rather than a string.

BASIC What are synthetic events? +

React's cross-browser wrapper around the browser's native event. They have the same interface as native events but work identically across all browsers.

BASIC How do you conventionally name boolean props? +

They are usually named like an assertion (e.g., `isOpen`, `hasLoaded`, `disabled`). Passing a boolean prop without a value defaults it to `true` (e.g., `

BASIC What is children in React? +

A special prop (`props.children`) that allows you to pass components as data to other components, just like nesting standard HTML elements.

BASIC What is a Fragment? +

`` or `<>` lets you group a list of children without adding extra nodes (like a `

`) to the DOM.

BASIC How do you conditionally render an element? +

Using JavaScript logical operators like `&&` (if true, render right side) or the ternary operator `condition ? true : false`.

BASIC Why is standard `class` attribute written as `className` in React? +

Because `class` is a reserved keyword in JavaScript. JSX is transformed into standard JS, requiring the use of the DOM API property `className`.

BASIC What is a controlled component? +

A form element whose value is controlled entirely by React state rather than the DOM.

BASIC What is an uncontrolled component? +

A component where the form data is handled by the DOM itself. You use a `ref` to access the value from the DOM directly when needed.

BASIC What does preventDefault() do? +

It prevents the default action that belongs to the event from occurring. Usually used in form submissions to stop the browser from reloading the page.

BASIC Why shouldn't you mutate state directly in React? +

Because React compares objects by reference. If you mutate state directly, the reference stays the same, and React doesn't know it needs to re-render. Always use the setter function.

BASIC What is prop drilling? +

The process of passing props from a higher-level component down to a lower-level component through many layers of intermediate components that don't actually need the data.

BASIC What is React Context? +

A way to pass data through the component tree without having to pass props down manually at every level. Useful for global data like themes or user authentication.

INTERMEDIATE Explain the dependency array in useEffect. +

If provided, the effect runs only when one of those dependencies has changed since the last render. If empty `[]`, it runs once on mount. If omitted entirely, it runs after EVERY render.

INTERMEDIATE What happens if you omit a changing variable from the useEffect dependency array? +

The effect might use stale closures (old values of the variable from a previous render), leading to bugs that are very difficult to track down.

INTERMEDIATE What is the cleanup function in useEffect? +

The function returned by the effect. It runs before the component unmounts AND before the effect runs again on a subsequent re-render. Crucial for removing event listeners or clearing timers.

INTERMEDIATE What is useCallback? +

A hook that returns a memoized callback function. It's used to prevent functions from being recreated on every render, which is useful when passing callbacks to optimized child components.

INTERMEDIATE What is useMemo? +

A hook that returns a memoized value. It's used to cache the results of expensive calculations between renders.

INTERMEDIATE When should you NOT use useMemo and useCallback? +

Don't use them indiscriminately. Memoization has a performance cost (memory allocation and dependency checking). Only use them if the child is wrapped in `React.memo` or if the calculation is genuinely expensive.

INTERMEDIATE What is React.memo()? +

A Higher Order Component (HOC) that memorizes the rendered output of a component, skipping re-renders if its props haven't changed (shallow comparison).

INTERMEDIATE What is useRef? +

A hook that returns a mutable ref object whose `.current` property is initialized to the passed argument. It persists for the full lifetime of the component and mutating it does NOT cause a re-render.

INTERMEDIATE List modern ways to manage global state in React. +

Zustand, Context API (for low-frequency updates), Redux Toolkit, Jotai, Recoil, and using Server State managers like React Query/TanStack Query.

INTERMEDIATE Why is using index as a key a bad idea? +

If the list order changes (sorting, deleting, inserting), the index stays the same for that position in the DOM. React will get confused, potentially matching wrong state to the wrong UI elements or failing to animate.

INTERMEDIATE What is the difference between element and component in React? +

An element is a plain object describing what you want to appear on the screen (`

` or ``). A component is a function or class that accepts input and returns a React element.

INTERMEDIATE What is the Custom Hook pattern? +

Extracting stateful logic from a component into a reusable JavaScript function whose name starts with "use". They can call other hooks internally.

INTERMEDIATE What is StrictMode in React? +

A tool for highlighting potential problems in an application. In development mode, it intentionally double-invokes components and lifecycle methods to help find side effects and deprecations.

INTERMEDIATE How do you lazy load a component in React? +

Using `React.lazy()` combined with `}>`. It allows you to dynamically import a component file only when it is actually needed to be rendered.

INTERMEDIATE What are higher-order components (HOCs)? +

A function that takes a component and returns a new component, wrapping it with additional injected functionality. Mostly replaced in modern React by custom hooks.

INTERMEDIATE What is a render prop? +

A technique for sharing code between components using a prop whose value is a function. The component uses that function to determine what to render. (e.g. ` } />`)

INTERMEDIATE What is a Portal in React? +

It provides a first-class way to render children into a DOM node that exists entirely outside the DOM hierarchy of the parent component (e.g., `createPortal(child, container)`). Perfect for Modals and Tooltips.

INTERMEDIATE What is the "forwardRef" API? +

A way to let a component take a `ref` they receive and pass it further down ("forward" it) to a child DOM element. Required if a parent needs to measure or focus an inner button of a child component.

INTERMEDIATE What is useReducer? +

An alternative to `useState`. It is usually preferable when you have complex state logic that involves multiple sub-values or when the next state depends heavily on the previous one.

INTERMEDIATE Why might a React update be batched? +

React 18+ automatically batches multiple state updates within the same event loop (even inside promises or timeouts) into a single re-render for better performance.

INTERMEDIATE What is the difference between shallow rendering and full DOM rendering in testing? +

Shallow rendering tests a component in isolation without rendering its children. Full DOM rendering mounts the full tree. Modern testing (RTL) strongly prefers full mounting.

INTERMEDIATE How does React Router work under the hood? +

It listens to changes in the browser's History API. When the URL changes, it calculates which `` components match the path and renders them, blocking the browser from doing a full page refresh.

INTERMEDIATE What is the Context Consumer vs useContext? +

useContext is the modern Hook approach used inside a functional component. Context.Consumer is the older render-prop approach required in class components.

INTERMEDIATE How do you handle API calls in React components natively? +

You traditionally use fetch/axios inside a `useEffect` with an empty dependency array to call the API on mount, updating state on resolution, while managing `isLoading` and `error` states manually.

INTERMEDIATE Why is TanStack Query (React Query) recommended over raw useEffect? +

It automatically handles caching, background fetching, retries, deduplication, and simplifies loading/error state management significantly over manual useEffects.

INTERMEDIATE What is error boundary in React? +

A classical component (using `componentDidCatch`) that catches JavaScript errors anywhere in its child component tree, logs those errors, and displays a fallback UI instead of crashing the whole app.

INTERMEDIATE What causes the "Warning: Cannot update a component while rendering a different component" error? +

It happens when you try to update the state of a parent component directly synchronously inside the render body of a child component, usually by accidentally invoking a prop function instead of passing it by reference.

INTERMEDIATE How do you handle routing protection (Protected Routes)? +

By creating a wrapper component that checks an authentication token. If valid, it returns the children. If invalid, it uses `` to redirect to the login page.

INTERMEDIATE What is code splitting? +

Splitting your code into multiple smaller bundles instead of one massive file. React `lazy()` and dynamic imports load those bundles on demand, accelerating initial page load.

INTERMEDIATE What is hydration in React? +

When React boots up on the client and attaches event listeners to the static HTML that was already sent by a server during Server-Side Rendering (SSR).

ADVANCED What is the React 19 Compiler? +

An optimizing compiler that automatically memorizes your React code, effectively removing the need for manual `useMemo` and `useCallback` by analyzing component data dependencies at build time.

ADVANCED What are React Server Components (RSC)? +

A new architecture where components render exclusively on the server at build time or request time. They have ZERO JavaScript sent to the client, cannot use hooks, but can directly access databases.

ADVANCED What is the difference between a Server Component and SSR? +

SSR renders HTML on the server, but still sends the JS code to the client to hydrate into interactive components. RSC NEVER sends its JS to the client at all, acting purely as structured data.

ADVANCED What are Server Actions (React 19)? +

A feature allowing you to define asynchronous functions that execute on the server. You can call these directly from client-side `

` or event handlers without configuring API routes.

ADVANCED What is useOptimistic (React 19)? +

A hook that lets you eagerly update the UI assuming a server action will succeed, creating instantaneous feedback, while automatically rolling back if the action fails.

ADVANCED What does the use() hook do (React 19)? +

A hook that lets you read the value of a Promise or Context. Unlike other hooks, `use()` can be called conditionally (inside an if-statement) and integrates deeply with Suspense.

ADVANCED What is useFormStatus (React 19)? +

A hook representing the pending state of an enclosing ``. It replaces the need to manually manage loading state variables when submitting forms via Actions.

ADVANCED What is useActionState (React 19)? +

A hook that manages state returned from a form Action. It takes an action and an initial state, returning the current state, a new action to dispatch, and a pending boolean.

ADVANCED What is Suspense for Data Fetching? +

Allows components to "pause" rendering while waiting for an asynchronous operation (like fetching data) to finish, letting a parent `` boundary render a fallback UI until the data is ready.

ADVANCED What is the useLayoutEffect hook? +

It's identical to useEffect, but it fires synchronously *after* all DOM mutations but *before* the browser repaints the screen. Used for reading layout dimensions from the DOM to avoid visual flicker.

ADVANCED What is the useSyncExternalStore hook? +

A specialized hook designed to let React safely subscribe to external data stores (like Redux or Zustand) in a way that is compatible with React's concurrent rendering features without tearing.

ADVANCED What is Concurrent React? +

A set of architectural changes allowing React to interrupt a heavy rendering task to handle high-priority events (typing), then resume the background rendering. Uses `useTransition` and `useDeferredValue`.

ADVANCED What does useTransition do? +

It lets you mark certain state updates as non-urgent (transitions). If an urgent update (typing) comes in, React interrupts the transition. The hook provides an `isPending` flag for UI feedback.

ADVANCED What does useDeferredValue do? +

It lets you defer updating a part of the UI that is not critical. E.g., you immediately update a search input value, but you defer the value passed to an expensive search results list.

ADVANCED Explain tearing in UI frameworks. +

Tearing happens when a UI framework yields rendering to the main thread, an external state store updates, and the framework resumes rendering with the NEW state, resulting in half the screen using old state and half using new state. `useSyncExternalStore` fixes this.

ADVANCED How does the Virtual DOM reconciliation algorithm actually work? +

It assumes two rules: 1. Two elements of different types will produce different trees (React tears down the old tree unconditionally). 2. Developers can hint at which child elements may be stable using `key` props.

ADVANCED What is the danger of Context API causing redundant re-renders? +

Any component consuming a Context re-renders if ANY value in that Context object changes. If you bundle high-frequency updates with static values, the entire tree re-renders unnecessarily. Solution: Split contexts or use Zustand.

ADVANCED Explain the Compound Component pattern implementation. +

You create a parent `
